source_paper_title: Near-Optimal Performance of Stochastic Model Predictive Control
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Shin_2026, title={Near-Optimal Performance of Stochastic Model Predictive Control}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0159}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0159}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Shin, Sungho and Na, Sen and Anitescu, Mihai}, year={2026}, month=jan }
context:
Page 7, Remark 5; and Page 13, Section 4 (Conclusions and Future Work)

Remark 5. One limitation of our analysis is the assumption that the exact distribution of uncertainties is known. In practice, the true distribution of these uncertainties may remain unknown, highlighting the importance of incorporating distributional robustness into the control policy. Recent work in a different context has demonstrated stability in expectation within the stochastic MPC framework when the Wasserstein distance between the true and nominal distributions is bounded [45]. Nevertheless, the dynamic regret analysis regarding the distributional robustness property remains an open question.

Our dynamic regret analysis assumes that the exact distribution of uncertainties is known in advance. Distributional robustness concerning stability properties has been established in recent work [45]. Analyzing dynamic regret under inexact knowledge of the distribution presents an intriguing avenue for future research.
title: Dynamic regret bounds for stochastic MPC under distributional ambiguity setting
background_latex:
Consider a discrete-time controlled stochastic system over stages \(t\in\{0,1,\dots,T\}\) with state \(x_t\in\mathbb{R}^{n_x}\) and control \(u_t\in\mathbb{R}^{n_u}\). Let \(\xi:=\{\xi_t\}_{t=0}^T\) be an exogenous stochastic process taking values in a measurable set \(\Xi\). The dynamics and stage cost depend on \(\xi_t\):
\[
 x_t = A(\xi_t)x_{t-1}+B(\xi_t)u_{t-1}+d(\xi_t),
\]
\[
\ell(x_t,u_t;\xi_t)=\tfrac12\begin{bmatrix}x_t\\u_t\end{bmatrix}^\top\begin{bmatrix}Q(\xi_t)&0\\0&R(\xi_t)\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}x_t\\u_t\end{bmatrix}-\begin{bmatrix}q(\xi_t)\\r(\xi_t)\end{bmatrix}^\top\begin{bmatrix}x_t\\u_t\end{bmatrix}.
\]
A (nonanticipative) policy chooses \(u_t\) as a measurable function of the history \(\xi_{0:t}\) (equivalently, of \((\xi_{0:t},x_{0:t})\) under known dynamics). Given initial data \(w_{-1}:=(x_{-1},u_{-1})\) and \(\xi_0\), the stochastic optimal control problem minimizes the conditional expected total cost
\[
J^\star(\xi_0;w_{-1}) := \min \mathbb{E}\Big[\sum_{t=0}^T \ell(x_t,u_t;\xi_t)\,\Big|\,\xi_0\Big]
\]
subject to the dynamics holding for all realizations and nonanticipativity.

Stochastic model predictive control (SMPC) with prediction horizon \(W\) computes at each time \(t\) an optimal nonanticipative plan over \(t,t+1,\dots,t+W\) for the conditional distribution of the future uncertainties, then implements only the first control and repeats at \(t+1\). Let \(J^{(W)}(\xi_0;w_{-1})\) denote the expected total cost under this closed-loop SMPC policy. The dynamic regret is
\[
\mathrm{Regret}(W):=J^{(W)}(\xi_0;w_{-1})-J^\star(\xi_0;w_{-1}).
\]

The paper's main analysis assumes the probability law of \(\xi\) is known exactly. In many applications, only an estimate (nominal distribution) is available, and the true distribution may deviate from the nominal one; one formalization measures this deviation by a statistical distance (e.g., Wasserstein distance) between the true and nominal distributions.
problem_definition_latex: Assume the SMPC policy is computed using a nominal distribution for the uncertainty process \(\xi\), while the realized system is driven by a possibly different true distribution (i.e., the distribution is not known exactly in advance). Derive a dynamic-regret bound for SMPC that is valid under such inexact knowledge of the distribution (a distributionally robust dynamic regret analysis), quantifying how \(\mathrm{Regret}(W)=J^{(W)}(\xi_0;w_{-1})-J^\star(\xi_0;w_{-1})\) depends on the distribution mismatch.

open_problem_quote:
Remark 5. One limitation of our analysis is the assumption that the exact distribution of uncertainties is known. In practice, the true distribution of these uncertainties may remain unknown, highlighting the importance of incorporating distributional robustness into the control policy. Recent work in a different context has demonstrated stability in expectation within the stochastic MPC framework when the Wasserstein distance between the true and nominal distributions is bounded [45]. Nevertheless, the dynamic regret analysis regarding the distributional robustness property remains an open question.

Our dynamic regret analysis assumes that the exact distribution of uncertainties is known in advance. Distributional robustness concerning stability properties has been established in recent work [45]. Analyzing dynamic regret under inexact knowledge of the distribution presents an intriguing avenue for future research.
subject_classification: stochastic control and model predictive control
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The open question asks for a general theoretical regret bound under distributional ambiguity, not a yes/no property for a finite instance class. There is no clearly specified conjectured inequality with fixed constants that could be efficiently falsified by searching over small instances.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can simulate SMPC on parametrized linear-quadratic systems and evaluate empirical regret under sampled true vs nominal distributions, providing an automatic fitness signal. However, turning this into a provable bound depends on symbolic/analytic reasoning beyond what a purely computational evolutionary loop can reliably certify.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
